revision.comment: Sync from repo need_translation/ [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24615006798|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Ame-no-Vakahiko]][[en:Ame no Wakahiko]][[ja:アメノワカヒコ]][[pl:Młodzian Potrafiący się Oderwać od Nieba]][[zh:天稚彥]]
{{Multiple issues| {{Page numbers needed|date=September 2021|qq=}}{{Lead too short|date=September 2021|qq=}} {{Expand language|langcode=ja|otherarticle=アメノワカヒコ|date=October 2021|qq=}} }}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Ame no Wakahiko | spouse = {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}} (wife) | parents =[[Amatsukunitama]] (father); {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944}}| god_of = God of grains<ref>{{cite web |title=天若日子 京都通百科事典 |website=京都通百科事典 |url=https://www.kyototuu.jp/Jinjya/MikotoAmenowakahiko.html |language=ja |access-date=2020-09-29|qq=}}</ref> | image = | gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | consort = {{ill|Amenohazuchi|qid=Q10940642}}; {{ill|Shitateruhime|qid=Q11307233}} | equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|qid=Q506610}}; {{ill|Hitokotonushi|qid=Q10864079}}; {{ill|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|qid=Q10949054}}; {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|qid=Q3090037}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|qid=Q11387080}}; {{ill|honoakari|qid=Q10940411}}; {{ill|Ōmononushi|qid=Q10936701}}; {{ill|Ōyamakui|qid=Q10935105}} }} '''Ame no Wakahiko''' (天稚彦 or 天若日子) is a god of grains and ''[[Amatsukami]]'' in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=天若日子{{!|qq=}}アメノワカヒコ{{!|qq=}}日本神話の世界|url=https://www.shinwanosekai.info/amenowakahiko.php|access-date=2020-09-29 |website=www.shinwanosekai.info|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=天若日子 京都通百科事典|url=https://www.kyototuu.jp/Jinjya/MikotoAmenowakahiko.html|access-date=2020-09-29|website=京都通百科事典|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> He is the son of [[Amatsukunitama]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home: Kami in Classic Texts: Ame no Wakahiko|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=46|access-date=2020-09-29|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp|qq=}}</ref> The ''{{ill|Ame no Wakahiko Monogatari|ja|天稚彦草子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko Monogatari|1=Ame no Wakahiko Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22125968}}'', one of the ''[[Otogi-zōshi]]'', is a ''{{ill|Monogatari|en|Monogatari|qid=Q1503147|lt=Monogatari}}'' about him. == Name == The name Ame no Wakahiko means "a young boy in [[Takamagahara|heaven]]," and he is thought to be an exceptionally handsome youth.<ref name=":2" /> == Mythology == === Sent to earth === In many versions, when [[Ame no Hohi]] did not send a word for three years, all the gods gathered up, and Ame no Wakahiko was one who was chosen to rule the earth. In many versions Amenowakahiko was given a bow.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame+no+Wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|qq=}}</ref> In some versions, however, Ame no Wakahiko is the son of Ame no Hohi and Ame no Wakahiko was sent to earth to look for him.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&dq=Ame+no+Wakahiko&pg=PA6|isbn = 9781438128023|title = Japanese Mythology a to Z|year = 2009|publisher = Infobase|qq=}}</ref> === Death === In some versions, Ame no Wakahiko fell in love with {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}}. Eight years later, after not receiving no report back, the gods sent a bird named Nakime down to earth to check in on him. Following the advice of a wise woman, Ame no Wakahiko used his bow to shoot the bird.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2008 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533262-9 |series=Handbooks of world mythology |location=Oxford |pages=114|qq=}}</ref> The bird was killed but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. [[Takamimusubi]] saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Ame no Wakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uAj_PQ8o40sC&q=Takamimusubi|title=The East|date=1992|publisher=East Publications, Incorporated|language=en|qq=}}</ref> Other versions state that [[Amaterasu]] was the one who shot the arrow back at him.<ref>{{cite book | last=Abe | first=C. | title=Impurity and Death: A Japanese Perspective | publisher=Universal Publishers | year=2003 | isbn=978-1-58112-195-7 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FlZxh5OaZxoC&pg=PA38 | access-date=2020-09-29 | page=38|qq=}}</ref> This myth is the origin of a Japanese saying about the returning arrow.<ref name=":3" /> It is likely also a mythicisation of attempts made by the [[:en:Yamato State|Yamato state]] to impose its authority over neighbouring states.<ref name=":3" /> === Funeral === After Ame no Wakahiko's death, his parents built a hut (喪屋 moya) for their son. [[Ajisukitakahikone]] went down to pay his respect. However, due to Ajisukitakahikone looking similar to Ame no Wakahiko he was mistaken to be Ame no Wakahiko brought back to life. Ajisukitakahikone was offended by this and destroyed the hut and kicked it. The hut landed in the land of Mino and became a mountain called Moyama.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_II|title = Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1| chapter=Book II |qq=}}</ref><ref name=":1" /> === Other tales === Another story of Ame no Wakahiko appears in Japanese medieval literature of the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} ([[Otogi-zōshi]]), in a narrative very similar to the [[:en:Aarne-Thompson-Uther Index|Aarne-Thompson-Uther]] tale type ATU 425, "{{ill|Animal as Bridegroom|en|Animal as Bridegroom|qid=Q108179360|lt=The Search for the Lost Husband}}",<ref>김환희 [Hwan Hee Kim]. "<구렁덩덩신선비>와 「아메와카히코조시」의 친연성에 관한 비교문학적인 고찰" [A Comparative Study of the Affinities between the Korean Folktale of “the Serpent Husband” and the Japanese Story of “Ame no Wakahiko-zōshi”: a Study of <ATU 425 The Search for the Lost Husband> tales in Korea and Japan]. In: 민족문화연구 no. 63 (2014): 123. doi: 10.17948/kcs.2014..63.123</ref> or to the Graeco-Roman story of ''{{ill|Cupid and Psyche|en|Cupid and Psyche|qid=Q135662|lt=Cupid and Psyche}}'', by writer {{ill|Apuleius|en|Apuleius|qid=Q170512|lt=Apuleius}}.<ref>{{ill|Noriko T. Reider|en|Noriko T. Reider|qid=Q111834841|lt=Reider, Noriko T.}} "A Demon in the Sky: The Tale of Ame no Wakahiko, a Japanese Medieval Story". In: ''Marvels & Tales'' 29, no. 2 (2015): 265-266. Accessed June 28, 2021. doi:10.13110/marvelstales.29.2.0265.</ref> The story is alternatively known as ''Ame no Wakahiko sōshi'' or ''Ame no Wakahiko monogatari'' (''The Tale of Ame no Wakahiko''), and serves as another etiological tale for the ''{{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|qid=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata}}'' festival.<ref>''Monsters, Animals, and Other Worlds: A Collection of Short Medieval Japanese Tales''. Edited by Keller Kimbrough and Haruo Shirane. New York Chichester, West Sussex: Columbia University Press, 2018. p. 166. {{ISBN|9780231184472|qq=}}. https://doi.org/10.7312/kimb18446-003</ref> According to professor Masako Sato, the calligraphy of the text indicates that its author is Emperor [[:en:Gohanazono|Gohanazono]],<ref>Satō, Masako. "Transforming an Ancient Myth into a Popular Medieval Tale". In: ''Japan on the Silk Road''. Leiden, The Netherlands: Brill, 2017. p. 339. doi: https://doi.org/10.1163/9789004274310_018</ref> while French curator {{ill|Jeannine Auboyer|en|Jeannine Auboyer|qid=Q28377863|lt=Jeannine Auboyer}} dated the manuscript to ca. 1450.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Auboyer |first1=Jeannine |title=The Oriental world: India and South-East Asia |date=1967 |publication-place= New York |publisher= McGraw-Hill |isbn= |page=151 |qq=}}</ref> In this tale, Prince Ame no Wakahiko takes the form of a serpent. One day, he delivers a letter to the maidservant of a wealthy man. The maidservant gives the letter to her master, who opens it: within, a command for the man to surrender his three daughters to the serpent in marriage, else it will kill his entire family. The man's two elder daughters refuse to marry the animal, but the youngest decides to be married to the snake. The letter also instructed the man to build a palace near a pond, which is to serve as the couple's residence after their marriage. Soon, the human woman is delivered to the palace to await for her snake husband. An enormous serpent emerges from the pond and talks to its bride, assuaging her fears and asking for her to cut off his head. The human bride does as intended "with the blade of a fingernail clipper", and a handsome man comes out of the snakeskin. The man hides the snakeskin in a Chinese box and they enjoy their marital life. He later reveals he is a heavenly deity named Dragon Prince (or Dragon King - a ''kairyūō''),<ref>Reider, Noriko T. "A Demon in the Sky: The Tale of Ame no Wakahiko, a Japanese Medieval Story". In: ''Marvels & Tales'' 29, no. 2 (2015): 265. Accessed June 28, 2021. doi:10.13110/marvelstales.29.2.0265.</ref> and that he must travel somewhere, and asks her to wait for his return. He also begs his wife not to open the chest, lest she will never see him again. His wife asks him what can she do to find his way to him, and he answers that she must seek a woman in Kyoto and buy a gourd. After some time, the wife is visited by her sisters, who discover that their brother-in-law, the snake, is in fact a handsome man. Spurred by envy, they convince their youngest sister to open the Chinese box.<ref>Nakawaki Hatsue. "Japanese Heroine Tales and the Significance of Storytelling in Contemporary Society". In: ''Re-Orienting the Fairy Tale: Contemporary Adaptations across Cultures''. Edited by Mayako Murai and Luciana Cardi. Detroit: Wayne State University Press. 2020. p. 152. {{ISBN|978-0-8143-4537-5|qq=}}.</ref> Only smoke comes out of the box, and later she learns he cannot return to her. She must, then, seek him out. She goes to Kyoto, buys a gourd and uses its vines to reach the heavenly realm.<ref>Rumpf, Fritz. “Über Japanische Märchen Hagoromo (Das Federkleid)”. In: ''T’oung Pao'' 33, no. 3/4 (1937): 236-237, 249. http://www.jstor.org/stable/4527134.</ref> Now on Heaven, the girl asks directions from a man in hunting robes (the {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|qid=Q313|lt=Evening Star}}), then from a man with a broom (the {{ill|Comet|en|Comet|qid=Q3559|lt=Comet}}), and finally from a cluster of people (the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|qid=Q13423|lt=Pleiades}}), who cannot seem to know him. The girl then finds a person on a palanquin, who directs her to a jeweled mansion built on azure stone.<ref>''Monsters, Animals, and Other Worlds: A Collection of Short Medieval Japanese Tales''. Edited by Keller Kimbrough and Haruo Shirane. New York Chichester, West Sussex: Columbia University Press, 2018. pp. 169-170. {{ISBN|9780231184472|qq=}}. https://doi.org/10.7312/kimb18446-003</ref> At last, the girl enters the mansion and finds her heavenly husband, but he changes her into objects (an armrest, then a fan or a pillow) to hide her from his father, an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|qid=Q730052|lt=Oni}}''. Eventually, the oni father-in-law discovers her and sets four tasks on his son's human wife: to herd a thousand cattle in one day and one night, to transport one million grains of rice from one granary to another, to stay in a warehouse full of centipedes, and in a warehouse full of snakes. She accomplishes all four tasks with her husband's assistance, since he gave her a magical sleeve. At the end of this tale, the oni father lets his son and the wife meet once a month, but she mistakes it for "once a year" and thus, the lovers can only reunite during the ''{{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|qid=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata}}''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Komatsu |first=Kazuhiko |title=[www.persee.fr/doc/ephe_0000-0002_2002_num_115_111_12068 Conférence de M. Kazuhiko Komatsu] |journal=École pratique des hautes études, Section des sciences religieuses. Annuaire |volume=111 |date=2002 |page=123|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Satō |first=Masako |chapter=Transforming an Ancient Myth into a Popular Medieval Tale |title=Japan on the Silk Road |place=Leiden, The Netherlands |publisher=Brill |date=2017 |pages=339–345 |doi=10.1163/9789004274310_018|isbn=9789004274310 |qq=}}.</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Reider |first=Noriko T. |title=A Demon in the Sky: The Tale of Ame no Wakahiko, a Japanese Medieval Story |journal=Marvels & Tales |volume=29 |issue=2 |date=2015 |pages=265–282 |doi=10.13110/marvelstales.29.2.0265|s2cid=161774066 |qq=}}. Accessed June 28, 2021.</ref><ref>Reider, Noriko T. "Tale of Ame no Wakahiko (Ame no Wakahiko Sōshi): A Demon in the Sky, a Maiden in Search of Her Husband". In: ''Seven Demon Stories from Medieval Japan''. Boulder, Colorado: University Press of Colorado, 2016. Accessed June 28, 2021. pp. 135-155. http://www.jstor.org/stable/j.ctt1g04zg4.9.</ref> == Shrines of worship == {{expand section|date=September 2021|qq=}} Ame no Wakahiko is enshrined at Abiko Shrine in {{ill|Aishō, Shiga|en|Aishō, Shiga|qid=Q1346806|lt=Aishō, Shiga}} and Iwakura Shrine in {{ill|Shinshiro|en|Shinshiro|qid=Q694923|lt=Shinshiro, Aichi}}.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web|title=安孫子神社（滋賀県豊郷駅）のアクセス・お参りの情報 ｜ホトカミ|url=https://hotokami.jp/area/shiga/Hmstr/Hmstrtr/Dpzyp/80970/|access-date=2020-09-29|website=ホトカミ|qq=}}</ref> == See also == * [[Kuni-yuzuri]] * {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko Monogatari|ja|天稚彦草子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko Monogatari|1=Ame no Wakahiko Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22125968}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} ==Further reading== * Jugon, Jean-Claude. "[https://www.france-japon.net/documents/La%20romance%20du%20sentiment%20et%20de%20la%20pensee.pdf?x36399 La romance du sentiment et de la pensée dans les légendes de Tanabata (Japon) et d’Éros-Psyché (Grèce), une interprétation transculturelle: «Oni» soit qui mal y pense!]". In: 論叢現代語・現代文化 2015 Vol.14 pp. 1–92. (in French) * Nüffer, Laura. “Humans and Non-Humans: Animal Bridegrooms and Brides in Japanese Otogizōshi.” In: ''A Cultural History of Fairy Tales in the Age of the Marvelous''. Edited by Suzanne Magnanini. London: Bloomsbury Academic Press, 2021. pp. 95–118. * ITO, Yuko. "[https://tsuru.repo.nii.ac.jp/?action=pages_view_main&active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&item_id=201&item_no=1&page_id=4&block_id=34 Origins and development of the two versions of "Ame no Wakahiko Soshi": a comparative study]". In: 都留文科大学研究紀要 [The Tsuru University Review], Mar/2007, volume 65, pp. 258–241. https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110007055993/en/; {{ISSN|0286-3774|qq=}}. DOI: info:doi/10.34356/00000195 (in Japanese) * Yasufuku, Junko. "[http://ir.lib.osaka-kyoiku.ac.jp/dspace/handle/123456789/6149 The Tale of Ame no Wakahiko and the Psychic Development of the Feminine]". In: ''Memoirs of Osaka Kyoiku University. IV, Education, pshychology, special education and physical culture'', Osaka Kyoiku University, Feb/1995, volume 43, no. 2, pp. 251–258. {{ISSN|0389-3472|qq=}}. URL: https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110000119291/en/ (in Japanese) {{Shizuri Faith|qq=}}
[[en:Ame no Wakahiko]]
[[en:Ame no Wakahiko]]<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[az:Ame-no-Vakahiko]] [[ja:アメノワカヒコ]] [[zh:天稚彥]] {{wikidata link|Q10940674}}
{{translated page|ja|アメノワカヒコ|version=104157393|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]] [[Category:Need translation]] == Japanese Wikipedia content == {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 天若日子 | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->{{jalink|天津神}} | 全名 = 天若日子（アメノワカヒコ） | 別名 = 天稚彦、天若日子命、天若日子神、天稚彦命、天稚彦神 | 別称 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->{{jalink|穀物|Q12117}}神 | 父 = {{jalink|天津国玉神|Q135113177}} | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 兄弟姉妹 = | 配偶者 = {{jalink|シタテルヒメ|Q11307233|下照比売}} | 子 = | 神社 = {{Plainlist| * {{jalink|大矢田神社|Q11438123|喪山天神社}} * 天王山祖霊神社 * 安孫子神社 * 天稚彦神社 * 天稚神社 * {{jalink|売布神社 (宝塚市)|Q3304192|賣布神社}} * {{jalink|倭文神社 (湯梨浜町)|Q245734|倭文神社}} }} | 記紀等 = {{jalink|古事記|Q813031}}、{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}} 等 | 関連氏族 = }} '''アメノワカヒコ'''（天若日子、天稚彦）は、{{jalink|日本神話|Q814158}}に登場する{{jalink|神 (神道)|Q524158|神}}。'''アメワカヒコ'''、'''アマノワカヒコ'''ともする。 == 概要 == {{jalink|葦原中国平定}}において、天津国玉神（アマツクニタマ）の子として登場する。天津国玉神の系譜の記述はない。 == 記述 == === 古事記 === {{jalink|葦原中国|Q1096244}}を平定するに当たって、遣わされた{{jalink|天之菩卑能命}}（アメノホヒ）が3年たっても戻って来ないので、次に天若日子が遣わされた。
しかし、天若日子は{{jalink|大国主神}}の娘下照比売（シタテルヒメ）と結婚し、葦原中国を得ようと企んで8年たっても{{jalink|高天原|Q1141974}}に戻らなかった。そこで{{jalink|天照大御神}}と{{jalink|高御産巣日神}}（タカミムスビ）は雉の鳴女（ナキメ）を遣して戻ってこない理由を尋ねさせた。すると、その声を聴いた{{jalink|天探女|Q10939684|天佐具売}}（アメノサグメ）が、不吉な鳥だから射殺すようにと天若日子に勧め、彼は遣わされた時に高皇産巣日神から与えられた弓矢（{{jalink|天羽々矢|Q10940644}}と{{jalink|天之麻迦古弓|Q30926812}}）で雉を射抜いた。
その矢は高天原まで飛んで行った。その矢を手にした高皇産巣日神（『古事記』では高木神）は、「天若日子に邪心があるならばこの矢に当たるように」と{{jalink|うけい|Q11260316|誓約}}をして下界に落とす。すると、その矢は寝所で寝ていた天若日子の胸に刺さり、彼は死んでしまった。
天若日子の死を嘆く下照比売の泣き声が天まで届くと、天若日子の父の天津国玉神は下界に降りて葬儀のため喪屋を建て八日八夜の{{jalink|殯|Q11547048}}をした。下照姫の兄の{{jalink|アヂスキタカヒコネ|Q506610|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}（アヂスキタカヒコネ）も弔いに訪れたが、彼が天若日子に大変よく似ていたため、天若日子の父と妻が「天若日子は生きていた」と言って抱きついた。すると阿遅鉏高日子根神は「穢らわしい死人と見間違えるな」と怒り、{{jalink|神度剣|Q30926808|神渡剣}}を抜いて喪屋を切り倒し、蹴り飛ばしてしまった。喪屋が飛ばされた先は{{jalink|美濃国|Q864156|美濃}}の{{jalink|藍見村|Q11622604|藍見}}の喪山だという。 == 考証 == 名前の「ワカヒコ」は若い男の意味である。これが神名ではなく普通名詞だったため、「神」「命」「尊」の尊称が付かないとする説がある。また、{{jalink|天津神}}に反逆したためであるとする説もある。
天若日子の喪屋は『古事記』では地上に作ったとあるが、『日本書紀』では疾風（はやち）に遺体を上げさせて、喪屋は天に作ったとある。
天若日子と{{jalink|アヂスキタカヒコネ|Q506610|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}がそっくりだったということで、本来同一の神であったとする説もある。すなわち、アメノワカヒコの死とアヂスキタカヒコネとしての復活であり、これは穀物が秋に枯れて春に再生する、または太陽が冬に力が弱まり春に復活する様子を表したものであるとする。 == 伝承・信仰 == 下照姫との恋に溺れて使命を放棄し、その罪によって亡くなるという悲劇的かつ反逆的な神として、民間では人気があった。{{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}の『{{jalink|うつほ物語|Q3023227}}』、『{{jalink|狭衣物語|Q17225521}}』などでは天若御子の名で、{{jalink|室町時代|Q334845}}の『{{jalink|御伽草子|Q82440}}』に収録されている『{{jalink|天稚彦草子|Q22125968}}』では天稚彦の名で登場し、いずれも美男子として描かれている。
天若日子を唆した天探女が「{{jalink|天邪鬼|Q2417460|アマノジャク}}」の元となったとする説があるが、天若日子の「天若」が「アマノジャク」とも読めることから、天若日子がアマノジャクだとする説もある。
穀物神として安孫子神社（{{jalink|愛知郡 (滋賀県)|Q1131097|愛知郡}}{{jalink|秦荘町|Q5680979}}）、下照姫の配神として賣布神社（{{jalink|宝塚市|Q823967}}）、倭文神社（{{jalink|東伯郡|Q1131949}}{{jalink|湯梨浜町|Q1156158}}）などに祀られているが、祀る神社は少ない。 == 祀る神社 == * 喪山天神社（{{jalink|岐阜県|Q131277}}{{jalink|美濃市|Q853835}}大矢田） - 祭神{{Unbulleted list|{{jalink|大矢田神社|Q11438123}}の境内社。}} * 天王山祖霊神社（岐阜県美濃市大矢田） - 主祭神 * 安孫子神社（{{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}愛知郡愛荘町安孫子） - 主祭神 * 天稚彦神社（滋賀県{{jalink|犬上郡|Q1131103}}{{jalink|豊郷町|Q937037}}高野瀬） - 主祭神 * 天稚神社（{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|南丹市|Q170421}}{{jalink|日吉町殿田|Q125421103}}向山） - 主祭神 * 賣布神社（{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}宝塚市売布山手町） - 配祀 * 倭文神社（{{jalink|鳥取県|Q133935}}東伯郡湯梨浜町） - 主祭神 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *{{jalink|日本の神の一覧|Q3240847}} *{{jalink|死者の書 (折口信夫)|Q11546988|死者の書}} *{{jalink|七夕#織女星と牽牛星の伝説|Q13423122|七夕}} *{{jalink|キジも鳴かずば|Q114797446}} == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/amewakahiko/ 天若日子 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=2479&shrname=%E2%98%85%E5%A4%A7%E7%9F%A2%E7%94%B0%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%98%85 大矢田神社詳細] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=2478&shrname=%E2%98%85%E5%A4%A9%E7%8E%8B%E5%B1%B1%E7%A5%96%E9%9C%8A%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%98%85 天王山祖霊神社詳細] * [https://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=885 神社紹介 > 滋賀県の神社 > 滋賀県神社庁]（安孫子神社） * [https://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=925 神社紹介 > 滋賀県の神社 > 滋賀県神社庁]（天稚彦神社） * [https://hiyoshikanko.com/?p=895 天稚神社の七夕まつり – 日吉町観光協会] * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6303005.html 賣布神社｜兵庫県神社庁 神社検索] * [https://tottori-jinjacho.jp/pages/315/ 倭文神社｜鳥取縣神社廳（公式ホームページ）] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[category:日本の神|あめのわかひこ]]
